---
title: "4.6 File, Scanner, and File Loops"
subtitle: "Using Text Files"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Using Text Files

### File and Scanner classes

A file can be connected to the program using the `File` and `Scanner` classes.

These classes must be imported from libraries:

::: {.tight-list}
- `Scanner` is in the `java.util` package
- `File` is in the `java.io` package
- `io` stands for **input/output** (输入/输出)
:::

## Opening a File

### File object

A file can be opened by creating a `File` object, using the name of the file or the complete path to the file as the argument of the constructor.

```java
import java.io.*;

File myFile = new File("data.txt");
```

After opening a file, the `Scanner` class can read data from the file line by line.

## IOException

### What if the file cannot be opened?

The `Scanner` constructor will throw a `FileNotFoundException` if it cannot find the file.

This is a type of `IOException`, a general error also thrown when input does not match expectations.

When an exception is thrown, the program stops executing and the exception is thrown back to the calling method.

## throws IOException

### Method header

Java uses `try/catch` blocks to handle exceptions, but you can use the `throws` keyword in the method header.

The `throws IOException` statement is added to the end of the method header.

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.io.*;
import java.util.*;

public class FileIO
{
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException
    {
        File myFile = new File("data.txt");
        Scanner scan = new Scanner(myFile);

        scan.close();
    }
}
```
:::

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: throws-exception-exercise`

Run the code below to see the error message.

Add `throws` and the correct exception to the end of the `main` method header to fix the error.

## FileIO Source Code

```java
import java.io.*;
import java.util.*;

public class FileIO
{
    public static void main(String[] args)
    {
        File myFile = new File("dictionary.txt");
        Scanner scan = new Scanner(myFile);
        System.out.println(
                "The first word in the dictionary file is: " + scan.nextLine());
        scan.close();
    }
}
```

## FileIO Test Targets

### Runestone checks

Expected output:

```text
The first word in the dictionary file is: a
```

The code must contain:

```java
throws IOException
```

## Reading in Data with Scanner

### Scanner methods

Once the file is opened, the data can be read using `Scanner` methods.

For example, `nextLine` reads the next line of input and returns it as a `String`.

::: {.tight-list}
- `Scanner(File f)` accepts a `File` for reading
- `String nextLine()` returns the next line of text
- `String next()` returns the next `String` up to whitespace
- `int nextInt()` returns the next `int`
:::

## Scanner Methods

### Numeric and stream methods

::: {.tight-list}
- `double nextDouble()` returns the next `double`
- `boolean nextBoolean()` returns the next `boolean`
- `boolean hasNext()` returns true if there is a next item to read
- `void close()` closes the input stream
:::

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: calculatorInput`

The code below reads in the 2 numbers below it and multiplies them.

Try changing the input below the code to decimal numbers like `2.5` and run to see the `InputMismatchException`.

Then change the code to use `nextDouble()` instead of `nextInt()` and change the variables to type `double`.

## calculatorInput Source Code

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class Multiply
{
     public static void main(String[] args)
     {
          Scanner scan = new Scanner(System.in);
          int num1 = scan.nextInt();
          int num2 = scan.nextInt();

          int result = num1 * num2;

          System.out.println(num1 + " x " + num2 + " = " + result);
          scan.close();
      }
}
```

## calculatorInput Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The input is:

```text
2 5
```

The tests check that the revised code contains:

```java
nextDouble()
double
```

## Scanner Whitespace

### Numeric methods and nextLine

The `nextInt()` and `nextDouble()` methods read in a number but leave any space or newline characters on the input stream.

This can cause problems if you have input to read in multiple lines.

Java programmers often add an extra call to `scan.nextLine();` to read in the rest of the line after reading in a number.

## Loop to Read in a File

### Use hasNext as the loop condition

A `while` loop is usually used to read in a file with multiple lines.

The loop can use `hasNext` as the loop condition to detect if the file still contains elements to read.

After the loop is finished reading the data, the `close` method from `Scanner` should be called to close the file.

```java
while (scan.hasNext())
{
    String line = scan.nextLine();
}
scan.close();
```

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: loop-read-file`

Complete the code below to read in all of the dictionary file using a `while` loop.

This code will also tell you how many lines are in the file.

## FileIOLoop Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.io.*;
import java.util.*;

public class FileIOLoop
{
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException
    {
        File myFile = new File("dictionary.txt");
        Scanner scan = new Scanner(myFile);
        int i = 0; // line count

        // 1. Add in the loop condition that checks if scan has another line of input
        while (         )
        {
            // 2. Read in the next line of the file
            String line;

            i++; // line count
         }

        System.out.println("This file has " + i + " lines.");

        scan.close();
    }
}
```
:::

## FileIOLoop Test Targets

### Runestone checks

Expected output:

```text
This file has 10000 lines.
```

The code must contain:

```java
nextLine()
hasNext()
```

## Save File Data into an Array

### Line by line

We can save a file line by line into an array.

In the `SpellChecker` class, we read the data file of words into a dictionary array with this code.

The source notes that the array size must be known before declaring the array.

```java
String[] dictionary = new String[10000];
int i = 0;
while(scan.hasNext())
{
     String line = scan.nextLine();
     dictionary[i] = line;
     i++;
}
```

## CSV Files

### Comma Separated Values

The following exercise reads in a data file about Pokemon and prints out the first 10 lines in the file.

This file has the extension `csv`, which stands for **Comma Separated Values**.

All spreadsheets can be saved as CSV text files, and spreadsheet software can easily open CSV files as spreadsheets.

## Coding Exercise

### `activecode:: read-pokemon-file`

Complete the code in the `main` method to:

::: {.tight-list}
- read in the first 10 lines of the pokemon file using the `Scanner` class
- save each line into the `pokemonLines` array
- print it out
- check that the line counter `i` is less than `10` in the loop condition
:::

## ReadData Starter

::: {.code-scroll}
```java
import java.io.*;
import java.util.*;

public class ReadData
{
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException
    {
        File myFile = new File("pokemon.csv");
        Scanner scan = new Scanner(myFile);
        String[] pokemonLines = new String[10];

        int i = 0;
        // 1. Add in the loop condition that checks if scan has another line of input
        //    and that i is less than 10.
        while (         )
        {
            // 2. Read in the next line of the file

            // 3. Assign the line to the ith element of the pokemonLines array

            // 4. Print out the line

            i++; // line count
         }
         scan.close();
   }
}
```
:::

## ReadData Test Targets

### Runestone checks

The output should contain at least 10 lines.

The tests check:

::: {.tight-list}
- assignment to `pokemonLines[i]`
- `hasNext()`
- `i < 10`
:::

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should include

::: {.tight-list}
- import `java.io.*` and `java.util.*` when using `File` and `Scanner`
- add `throws IOException` to the method header when using file input this way
- use `nextLine()`, `nextDouble()`, or other Scanner methods according to input type
- close the `Scanner` after reading
- use `hasNext()` as the loop condition when reading multiple lines
- store file lines into an array with an index counter
:::

## End

### 4.6 Part 1 complete

Next: splitting CSV strings and Pokemon images.
